title: OSI Reference Model — 7 Layers
examTip: Mnemonic (top→bottom): 'All People Seem To Need Data Processing'. PDUs: Data|Data|Data|Segment|Packet|Frame|Bits. Each layer adds a header (encapsulation) going down, removes it going up (decapsulation).

layers.name: Application
layers.protocols: HTTP  HTTPS  FTP  SMTP  DNS  Telnet  SNMP  POP3

layers.name: Presentation
layers.protocols: SSL/TLS  JPEG  MPEG  GIF  ASCII  EBCDIC  Encryption

layers.name: Session
layers.protocols: NetBIOS  RPC  SQL  NFS  PPTP  L2TP

layers.name: Network
layers.protocols: IP (v4/v6)  ICMP  OSPF  BGP  RIP  ARP

layers.name: Physical
layers.protocols: RS-232  DSL  SONET  Fiber Optic  802.11 PHY  USB

theory:
The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model is a conceptual framework developed by ISO (International Organization for Standardization) in 1984 to standardize communication functions in a network. It divides the communication process into 7 ordered layers, each providing specific services to the layer above it and receiving services from the layer below.

Layer 7 — Application: The topmost layer; provides network services directly to end-user applications (browsers, email clients). Protocols: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet, SNMP.

Layer 6 — Presentation: Translates data between application format and network format. Handles encryption/decryption (SSL/TLS), compression, and character encoding (ASCII, EBCDIC). Data format conversion (JPEG, MPEG, GIF).

Layer 5 — Session: Establishes, manages, and terminates communication sessions between applications. Provides dialog control (half-duplex or full-duplex) and synchronization via checkpoints. Protocols: NetBIOS, RPC, NFS.

Layer 4 — Transport: Provides end-to-end reliable data transfer between processes. TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) provides connection-oriented, reliable, ordered delivery with flow control and error recovery. UDP (User Datagram Protocol) provides connectionless, fast, unreliable delivery. PDU: Segment. Port numbers identify processes.

Layer 3 — Network: Handles logical addressing (IP addresses) and routing of data packets between different networks. Routers operate at this layer. IP fragmentation occurs here. PDU: Packet. Protocols: IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP, RIP.

Layer 2 — Data Link: Handles physical addressing (MAC addresses), framing, and error detection (CRC). Divided into LLC (Logical Link Control) and MAC (Media Access Control) sublayers. Switches and bridges operate here. PDU: Frame.

Layer 1 — Physical: Transmits raw bits over the physical medium — electrical signals on copper wire, light pulses on fiber optic, or radio waves in wireless. Defines voltage levels, connector types, cable specifications, and bit timing. PDU: Bits. Devices: Hub, Repeater.

Data Encapsulation: As data travels down the layers from sender, each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer). The reverse (decapsulation) occurs at the receiver.
keyPoints: 7 layers: Application(7) → Presentation(6) → Session(5) → Transport(4) → Network(3) → Data Link(2) → Physical(1)
PDU names: Data | Data | Data | Segment | Packet | Frame | Bits (top to bottom)
TCP/UDP operate at Layer 4 (Transport); IP operates at Layer 3 (Network)
Devices: Router=L3, Switch=L2, Hub=L1, Firewall=L3/L4, Gateway=L7
Encapsulation (sender) adds headers going down; Decapsulation (receiver) removes them going up
useCases: Network troubleshooting — identifying at which layer a communication failure occurs
Protocol design — ensuring new protocols fit within the standardized layer model
Vendor interoperability — OSI model allows devices from different manufacturers to communicate
